sourceMovie.title: Peninsula
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Peninsula (2020) as a horror theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The horror and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Train to Busan
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Train to Busan (2016) is the kind of film Peninsula fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Badland Hunters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Badland Hunters shares Peninsula's gift for horror — the 2024 film delivers action without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Extinction
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Peninsula compelling — the horror, the action — shows up in Extinction (2015) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Seoul Station
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of Peninsula is alive in Seoul Station (2016), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: John Wick: Chapter 4
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: John Wick: Chapter 4 (2023) operates in the same action as Peninsula, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Resident Evil: Apocalypse
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Resident Evil: Apocalypse (2004) belongs to the same conversation as Peninsula — both anchor their horror in action emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Death Race
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Peninsula's action stayed with you, Death Race (2008) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: [REC]²
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Peninsula excels in horror, [REC]² (2009) doubles down on thriller to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Repo Men
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Repo Men (2010) is the natural next step after Peninsula: same action DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and thriller elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Long Walk
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Peninsula who respond to its horror will find The Long Walk (2025) immediately familiar. Both films share a thriller sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

faq.question: What should I watch after Peninsula?
faq.answer: Try Train to Busan, Badland Hunters, Extinction—each shares DNA with Peninsula while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Peninsula?
faq.answer: Peninsula (2020) is best described as Horror, Action, Thriller, Adventure. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Peninsula part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Train to Busan Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
